title: Post-Thyroidectomy Levothyroxine Start in a Patient with Stable CAD
subtitle: Why a euthyroid-pre-op patient with revascularized, asymptomatic CAD gets near-full replacement, not 'start low, go slow' — dose, TSH target, and the 6-week check

relatedChapters.title: Severe Hypothyroidism with an Incidental Positive ANA

caseDescription: A 50 kg man undergoes total thyroidectomy. He was euthyroid pre-operatively and has coronary artery disease that is stable: prior PTCA with a stent, normal pre-op coronary angiogram, no angina, asymptomatic. The question is the starting levothyroxine (LT4) dose. The reflex 'start low, go slow' (25 mcg titrated up) is the wrong default here: it is for elderly patients, long-standing untreated hypothyroidism, or unstable angina/recent ACS — not for a euthyroid patient whose body is calibrated to normal thyroid hormone and whose coronary disease is stable and revascularized. Because total thyroidectomy removes all thyroid function and T4 has a ~7-day half-life, a 'start low' strategy manufactures weeks of iatrogenic hypothyroidism, which itself worsens lipids, blood pressure, vascular resistance, and cardiac risk. The rational choice is near-full replacement (~1.5 mcg/kg ≈ 75 mcg/day), with the TSH target set by the surgical indication (benign 0.5–2.5; risk-stratified suppression for differentiated thyroid cancer), avoiding over-suppression in CAD because suppressed TSH raises atrial fibrillation and cardiac-mortality risk. Confirm with a TSH at 6 weeks (steady state) and titrate by 12.5–25 mcg.
keyFindings: Euthyroid pre-op + stable, revascularized, asymptomatic CAD with normal pre-op CAG → near-full replacement is appropriate, not 'start low, go slow'
'Start low' (25 mcg) is reserved for elderly, long-standing untreated hypothyroidism, or unstable angina/recent ACS
Total thyroidectomy = zero residual function; full replacement ~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg (≈75 mcg for 50 kg), higher than Hashimoto's (residual function)
Prolonged hypothyroidism is itself pro-atherogenic and bad for CAD (↑LDL, ↑diastolic BP, ↑SVR, ↓contractility) — a reason to replace promptly
TSH target by indication: benign 0.5–2.5; low-risk DTC 0.5–2.0; intermediate/high-risk DTC suppression — but avoid over-suppression in CAD (AF, mortality)
Check TSH at 6 weeks (T4 t½ ~7 days → steady state in 5–6 weeks); adjust by 12.5–25 mcg; separate LT4 from calcium/iron/PPI by ~4 h
finalDiagnosis: Athyreotic state after total thyroidectomy in a patient with stable revascularized CAD — start near-full LT4 replacement (~75 mcg/day for 50 kg), set the TSH target by the surgical indication, avoid over-suppression, and re-check TSH at 6 weeks
learningObjectives: Decide between 'start low, go slow' and near-full LT4 replacement after thyroidectomy based on pre-op thyroid status, age, and CAD stability
Calculate and individualize the LT4 dose (athyreotic ~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg) and set a TSH target appropriate to the surgical indication while protecting a CAD patient from over-suppression
Structure follow-up: the 6-week steady-state TSH, titration steps, absorption interactions, and the cardiac red flags that prompt dose reduction

items.title: Why 'start low, go slow' is the wrong default for this patient
items.content: 'Start low, go slow' (LT4 25 mcg titrated up over weeks) is a genuine strategy — but for specific patients: the elderly (roughly >70), those with long-standing untreated hypothyroidism whose physiology has adapted to a low-hormone state, and those with unstable angina or recent acute coronary syndrome. This patient fits none of those. He was euthyroid until the operation, so his cardiovascular and metabolic systems are calibrated to normal thyroid hormone, not adapted to deficiency; and his CAD is stable — revascularized, normal pre-op coronary angiogram, no angina. Applying the cautious protocol here doesn't add safety; it manufactures weeks of iatrogenic hypothyroidism with no offsetting benefit. Match the strategy to the patient, not to the diagnosis label 'CAD'.
keyPoints: 'Start low' is for the elderly, long-standing untreated hypothyroidism, or unstable angina/recent ACS.
This patient was euthyroid pre-op and has stable, revascularized, asymptomatic CAD.
Stable CAD ≠ unstable CAD — the cautious protocol adds no safety here.

items.title: Why prolonged hypothyroidism is itself bad for the coronary patient
items.content: The fear behind 'start low' is that thyroid hormone raises myocardial oxygen demand and could provoke ischaemia. That is real in unstable disease — but the opposite error is overlooked: hypothyroidism is itself pro-atherogenic and cardiotoxic. It raises LDL cholesterol and homocysteine, raises diastolic blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance, reduces cardiac contractility, can cause pericardial effusion, and creates a pro-coagulant tendency. So in a stable, revascularized patient, deliberately inducing weeks of hypothyroidism trades a small, largely theoretical ischaemic risk for a concrete worsening of his atherosclerotic risk profile. Promptly restoring euthyroidism is the cardioprotective move; under-treatment is not the 'safe' option it appears to be.
keyPoints: Hypothyroidism raises LDL, homocysteine, diastolic BP, and systemic vascular resistance.
It also reduces contractility and is pro-coagulant — net pro-atherogenic.
In stable CAD, inducing hypothyroidism worsens risk more than prompt replacement does.

items.title: How to calculate the starting dose: the 1.6 mcg/kg rule and its modifiers
items.content: The classic full-replacement estimate is ~1.6 mcg/kg/day of ideal body weight: for 50 kg that is 80 mcg. Treat this as a guide, not a law. Athyreotic (post-total-thyroidectomy) patients genuinely need full replacement, typically 1.5–1.7 mcg/kg. Several factors lower the requirement — older age (~20–25% less over 60), female sex, lean body mass, and residual thyroid function (as in Hashimoto's) — while others raise it: pregnancy (↑25–50%), malabsorption, oestrogen therapy, and bariatric surgery. For this lean 50 kg man with stable CAD, starting at ~1.5 mcg/kg (≈75 mcg) rather than the full 1.6 mcg/kg (80 mcg) is a reasonable, modestly individualized starting point, with the final dose set by TSH. The difference between 75 and 80 mcg is small — the substantive decision is full-ish replacement versus 25 mcg, not 75 versus 80.
keyPoints: Full replacement ≈1.6 mcg/kg IBW (80 mcg for 50 kg); athyreotic patients need ~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg.
Lower with age, female sex, lean mass, residual function; higher with pregnancy, malabsorption, oestrogen.
Start ~75 mcg (1.5 mcg/kg) here; the big call is full-ish dose vs 25 mcg, not 75 vs 80.

items.title: Why post-total-thyroidectomy needs more than Hashimoto's hypothyroidism
items.content: Dosing differs by the amount of residual thyroid tissue. In primary hypothyroidism from Hashimoto's, onset is gradual and 5–30% of gland function often persists, so the average requirement is lower (~1.3–1.5 mcg/kg) and a low-start, slow-titration approach is physiologically fine because the body has already adapted to a low-hormone state. After total thyroidectomy there is zero residual function and the loss is abrupt in a previously euthyroid patient, so the requirement is higher (~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg) and full replacement is the logical start. Hemithyroidectomy sits in between — function loss is variable, many need 50–100 mcg and some need nothing, so the practical rule there is to check TSH at ~6 weeks before deciding whether to replace at all.
keyPoints: Hashimoto's: residual function (~5–30%), ~1.3–1.5 mcg/kg, slow titration acceptable.
Post-total-thyroidectomy: zero residual, ~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg, full replacement logical.
Hemithyroidectomy: variable — check TSH at ~6 weeks before deciding to replace.

items.title: How the surgical indication sets the TSH target — and why CAD caps suppression
items.content: There is no single TSH target; it depends on why the thyroid was removed. For benign disease (multinodular goitre, Graves') aim for low-normal 0.5–2.5 mIU/L. For differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) the target is risk-stratified: low-risk ~0.5–2.0, intermediate-risk mild suppression ~0.1–0.5, high-risk full suppression <0.1. The crucial overlay in this patient is his CAD: a chronically suppressed TSH (<0.1) — i.e. subclinical thyrotoxicosis from over-replacement — raises the risk of atrial fibrillation, bone loss, and cardiac mortality. So avoid over-suppression unless the oncological indication truly demands it; if a high-risk cancer and CAD genuinely collide, that is an explicit endocrinology–cardiology risk–benefit conversation, aiming as low as the cancer needs but as high as the heart allows.
keyPoints: Benign: TSH 0.5–2.5. DTC: risk-stratified (low 0.5–2.0; intermediate 0.1–0.5; high <0.1).
Suppressed TSH (over-replacement) raises AF, bone-loss, and cardiac-mortality risk.
In CAD, avoid over-suppression unless oncologically mandated — a joint risk–benefit decision.

items.title: Why the first TSH is at 6 weeks, and how to titrate from it
items.content: Levothyroxine has a long half-life (~7 days), so plasma levels — and the pituitary TSH response — take about 5–6 half-lives to reach steady state. Checking TSH before ~6 weeks gives a misleadingly high value and tempts premature, excessive dose increases. So set the first TSH (with free T4) at 6 weeks. Then titrate in small steps: if TSH is high, increase LT4 by 12.5–25 mcg; if at target, hold; if low or suppressed, decrease by 12.5–25 mcg. Re-check ~6 weeks after any change. Once stable, TSH every 6–12 months lifelong. If TSH stays high despite an adequate dose, interrogate adherence, dose timing, and absorption (food, coffee, calcium/iron/PPI) before simply escalating the dose.
keyPoints: T4 t½ ~7 days → steady state in 5–6 weeks; first TSH at 6 weeks (with free T4).
Titrate by 12.5–25 mcg; re-check 6 weeks after each change; then 6–12-monthly once stable.
Persistently high TSH on adequate dose → check adherence/timing/absorption before escalating.

items.title: How to take levothyroxine and what blunts its absorption
items.content: Levothyroxine is absorbed best on an empty stomach: take it with water 30–60 minutes before breakfast (or at bedtime, ≥3 hours after the last meal), at the same time daily, and keep coffee/tea away for about an hour. Several common co-medications bind LT4 or raise gastric pH and must be separated by ~4 hours: calcium carbonate/citrate, iron, magnesium and aluminium antacids, sucralfate, cholestyramine (the worst offender), sevelamer, and proton-pump inhibitors. This matters here because post-thyroidectomy patients are routinely on calcium (± calcitriol) for hypocalcaemia risk — that calcium must not be co-swallowed with the LT4. Separately, enzyme inducers (phenytoin, carbamazepine, rifampicin) increase LT4 metabolism, and oestrogen raises thyroxine-binding globulin, both increasing dose needs.
keyPoints: Empty stomach, 30–60 min before breakfast (or bedtime ≥3 h post-meal); consistent timing.
Separate by ~4 h: calcium, iron, antacids, sucralfate, cholestyramine, sevelamer, PPIs.
Enzyme inducers (phenytoin/carbamazepine/rifampicin) and oestrogen raise the dose requirement.

items.title: How to monitor the cardiac patient and which red flags cut the dose
items.content: Even with a justified full-dose start, watch the heart. Counsel the patient to report chest pain, palpitations, or sweating promptly. Cardiac red flags — new or worsening angina, new palpitations or arrhythmia (especially atrial fibrillation), worsening dyspnoea, or ischaemic ECG changes — should trigger dose reduction (e.g. down to 50 mcg), an ECG and troponin if chest pain, cardiology review, and a repeat TSH in 2–4 weeks. Over-replacement also shows up as hyperthyroid symptoms: tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss, anxiety, insomnia, diarrhoea — reduce the dose by ~25 mcg. Continue his cardiac medications (aspirin, statin, beta-blocker) throughout, and check serum calcium in the early post-thyroidectomy period for hypocalcaemia.
keyPoints: Red flags: new/worse angina, new arrhythmia/AF, worsening dyspnoea, ischaemic ECG → reduce dose, ECG/troponin, cardiology.
Hyperthyroid symptoms (tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss) signal over-replacement → cut ~25 mcg.
Continue aspirin/statin/beta-blocker; check early post-op calcium for hypocalcaemia.

items.stem: A 50 kg man undergoes total thyroidectomy for multinodular goitre. He was euthyroid pre-op and has stable CAD (prior PTCA/stent, normal pre-op coronary angiogram, no angina). What is the best starting dose of levothyroxine?
options: 25 mcg/day, titrated up every 2–4 weeks
Withhold LT4 and recheck TSH at 6 weeks before starting
Near-full replacement, about 75 mcg/day (~1.5 mcg/kg)
150 mcg/day
items.explanation: He was euthyroid pre-op (no adaptation to a low-hormone state) and his CAD is stable and revascularized, so near-full replacement (~1.5 mcg/kg ≈ 75 mcg) is appropriate and avoids weeks of iatrogenic hypothyroidism. 'Start low, go slow' is for the elderly, long-standing untreated hypothyroidism, or unstable angina/ACS; withholding replacement after a total thyroidectomy just guarantees hypothyroidism; 150 mcg is supraphysiologic for 50 kg.

items.stem: After starting levothyroxine, when should the first TSH be checked, and why?
options: At 1 week, because TSH responds immediately to replacement
At 6 weeks, because steady state takes 5–6 half-lives of LT4
At 6 months, because TSH is slow to change
Only if the patient becomes symptomatic
items.explanation: Levothyroxine's half-life is ~7 days, so it takes about 5–6 half-lives (~5–6 weeks) to reach steady state; checking earlier gives a falsely high TSH and tempts premature over-titration. Six weeks (with free T4) is the standard first check.

items.stem: The thyroidectomy was for benign multinodular goitre and the patient has CAD. What is the most appropriate TSH target?
options: Fully suppressed, <0.1 mIU/L
0.1–0.5 mIU/L (mild suppression)
0.5–2.5 mIU/L (low-normal)
5–10 mIU/L
items.explanation: For a benign indication the target is low-normal, 0.5–2.5 mIU/L. Suppressing TSH (<0.1) is reserved for risk-stratified thyroid cancer and is specifically undesirable in CAD because chronic subclinical thyrotoxicosis raises atrial fibrillation and cardiac-mortality risk; a frankly high TSH would leave him hypothyroid.

items.stem: This post-thyroidectomy patient is also prescribed calcium carbonate for hypocalcaemia risk. What is the key counselling point about taking it with levothyroxine?
options: Take the calcium together with the LT4 to improve compliance
Separate the calcium from the LT4 by about 4 hours
Calcium has no effect on LT4 absorption
Stop the calcium because it makes LT4 toxic
items.explanation: Calcium (like iron, antacids, sucralfate, cholestyramine, sevelamer, and PPIs) markedly reduces levothyroxine absorption, so it must be separated by about 4 hours. The calcium is needed for post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia risk — the fix is timing, not stopping it.

items.stem: Why does a post-total-thyroidectomy patient generally need a higher per-kilogram levothyroxine dose than a typical patient with Hashimoto's hypothyroidism?
options: Levothyroxine is chemically different after surgery
Surgery permanently impairs LT4 absorption
There is zero residual thyroid function, so full replacement is required
Post-surgical patients metabolize LT4 faster
items.explanation: Total thyroidectomy leaves no residual thyroid tissue, so the full daily output must be replaced (~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg). Hashimoto's patients often retain 5–30% of gland function, so they need somewhat less (~1.3–1.5 mcg/kg). The drug, its absorption, and its metabolism are unchanged by surgery.

items.stem: Three weeks after starting levothyroxine 75 mcg, the patient develops new exertional angina and palpitations. What is the most appropriate immediate response?
options: Increase LT4 to overcome residual hypothyroidism
Reduce the LT4 dose, obtain ECG/troponin, and arrange cardiology review
Stop all cardiac medications
Continue unchanged and recheck TSH at 6 months
items.explanation: New angina or arrhythmia on levothyroxine is a cardiac red flag: reduce the dose (e.g. to 50 mcg), evaluate ischaemia with ECG and troponin if there is chest pain, and involve cardiology, then re-titrate slowly once stable. Increasing the dose would worsen myocardial oxygen demand, and his cardiac medications should be continued.

items.statement: Leaving this patient hypothyroid for several weeks by starting a very low levothyroxine dose is harmless to his coronary disease.
items.explanation: False. Hypothyroidism raises LDL cholesterol, diastolic blood pressure, and systemic vascular resistance, reduces contractility, and is pro-coagulant — all adverse for coronary disease. Inducing weeks of hypothyroidism is not the 'safe' option.

items.statement: 'Start low, go slow' levothyroxine initiation is specifically indicated for the elderly and for patients with unstable angina or recent ACS.
items.explanation: True. The cautious low-start protocol is for the elderly, long-standing untreated hypothyroidism, and active/unstable ischaemia — not for a previously euthyroid patient with stable, revascularized, asymptomatic CAD.

items.statement: TSH should be rechecked one week after starting levothyroxine to confirm the dose is correct.
items.explanation: False. Because LT4's half-life is ~7 days, steady state takes about 5–6 weeks; a TSH at one week is misleadingly high. The first TSH should be at ~6 weeks.

items.statement: In a patient with CAD, chronically suppressing TSH below 0.1 mIU/L is harmless as long as free T4 is normal.
items.explanation: False. A chronically suppressed TSH (subclinical thyrotoxicosis from over-replacement) increases the risk of atrial fibrillation and cardiac mortality; suppression should be reserved for risk-stratified thyroid cancer, and even then weighed against cardiac risk.

items.statement: Calcium and iron supplements can be taken at the same time as levothyroxine without affecting its absorption.
items.explanation: False. Calcium and iron (and antacids, sucralfate, cholestyramine, sevelamer, PPIs) significantly reduce levothyroxine absorption and must be separated from it by about 4 hours.

items.assertion: A previously euthyroid patient undergoing total thyroidectomy can generally be started on near-full levothyroxine replacement even with stable CAD.
items.reason: Because the patient was euthyroid pre-operatively, the cardiovascular system is calibrated to normal thyroid hormone levels and has not adapted to a hypothyroid state.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion: a euthyroid patient's physiology is already accustomed to normal thyroid hormone, so there is no adapted hypothyroid state requiring gradual re-introduction — provided the CAD is stable, near-full replacement is appropriate and avoids harmful hypothyroidism.

items.assertion: In a CAD patient on levothyroxine for benign disease, the TSH should not be driven below 0.1 mIU/L.
items.reason: A suppressed TSH reflects subclinical thyrotoxicosis, which increases the risk of atrial fibrillation and cardiac mortality.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion: over-replacement to a suppressed TSH produces subclinical thyrotoxicosis, raising AF and cardiac-mortality risk, so for a benign indication in CAD the target is low-normal (0.5–2.5), not suppressed.

items.question: Why can a Hashimoto's patient be started on a low levothyroxine dose and titrated slowly, while a total-thyroidectomy patient should usually start near full replacement?
items.answer: It comes down to residual function and adaptation. In Hashimoto's, hypothyroidism develops gradually over years, the gland often retains 5–30% of function, and the body has already adapted to lower hormone levels — so a low start is physiologically tolerated and slow titration causes no abrupt deficit. After total thyroidectomy there is no residual tissue and the loss is sudden in someone who was euthyroid moments before; their physiology is calibrated to normal hormone, not to deficiency. Starting low there manufactures an abrupt, avoidable hypothyroid state, so near-full replacement (~1.5–1.7 mcg/kg) is the logical start unless an unstable cardiac contraindication forces caution.
keyPoints: Hashimoto's: gradual onset + residual function → body adapted, low start tolerated.
Total thyroidectomy: zero residual + abrupt loss in a euthyroid patient → near-full replacement.
The deciding factors are residual function and prior adaptation, not the drug itself.

items.question: How should you follow up and titrate levothyroxine after starting 75 mcg in this patient?
items.answer: See him clinically at 1–2 weeks for chest pain/palpitations and (early post-op) serum calcium. Draw the first TSH with free T4 at 6 weeks, once steady state is reached. Then titrate against the indication-appropriate target: if TSH is high, increase by 12.5–25 mcg; if at target, hold; if low or suppressed, decrease by 12.5–25 mcg — re-checking ~6 weeks after each change. Once stable, monitor TSH every 6–12 months for life. Throughout, treat new angina, arrhythmia, or hyperthyroid symptoms as a cue to reduce the dose and reassess, and if TSH stays high on an adequate dose, check adherence, timing, and absorption interactions before escalating.
keyPoints: Clinical review at 1–2 weeks (+ early calcium); first TSH/free T4 at 6 weeks.
Titrate by 12.5–25 mcg to the indication-set target; recheck 6 weeks after changes; then 6–12-monthly.
New cardiac or hyperthyroid symptoms → reduce dose; persistent high TSH → check adherence/absorption first.

items.title: How settled is this approach — strengths, limitations, and what is individualized
items.content: Strength: the core call is well-supported — for a younger, previously euthyroid patient with stable, revascularized, asymptomatic CAD, ATA hypothyroidism guidance (Jonklaas et al., Thyroid 2014) supports full or near-full replacement rather than reflexive low-dose titration, and the harms of suppression in cardiac patients are well described. Limitations and doubt-flags: the precise number (75 vs 80 mcg) is a minor, individualized choice, not a hard threshold — the final dose is always set by TSH at steady state. 'Stable CAD' is doing a lot of work in this recommendation: if there were active angina, a recent ACS, a critical un-revascularized lesion, or advanced age, the calculus shifts toward a lower, slower start. The TSH target is entirely indication-dependent and, for differentiated thyroid cancer colliding with CAD, becomes an explicit endocrinology–cardiology trade-off rather than a fixed value. Finally, this assumes good adherence and no major malabsorption or interacting drugs; verify those before attributing a high TSH to under-dosing.
keyPoints: Solid: near-full replacement for euthyroid-pre-op, stable-CAD patients (ATA 2014); suppression harms in CAD.
Individualized/flagged: 75 vs 80 mcg is minor; 'stable CAD' is the key qualifier — active ischaemia changes the plan.
TSH target is indication-dependent; cancer-plus-CAD is a joint risk–benefit decision, and adherence/absorption must be excluded.

items.assertion: A Hashimoto's patient can be started on a low levothyroxine dose and titrated slowly, while a total-thyroidectomy patient should usually start near full replacement.
items.reason: Because Hashimoto's patients retain residual thyroid function and have gradual adaptation, whereas total-thyroidectomy patients experience abrupt hormone loss with no residual function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are correct, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Hashimoto's patients tolerate slow titration due to preserved function and prior adaptation, while thyroidectomy patients require immediate full replacement to avoid abrupt hypothyroidism.

items.stem: A patient is started on levothyroxine 75 mcg. Which approach is most appropriate for follow-up and dose titration?
options: A) Clinical review at 1–2 weeks; first TSH/free T4 at 6 weeks; titrate by 12.5–25 mcg based on TSH results; monitor TSH every 6–12 months once stable
B) Check TSH at 2 weeks; titrate by 25–50 mcg if TSH is elevated; monitor TSH annually
C) Initiate TSH monitoring at 4 weeks; adjust dose by 50 mcg increments if TSH remains high; monitor every 3–6 months
D) Perform TSH testing at 12 weeks; titrate based on TSH suppression; monitor TSH every 1–2 years
items.explanation: Initial clinical review and serum calcium assessment should occur 1–2 weeks post-initiation. First TSH/free T4 testing is performed at 6 weeks to allow steady-state attainment. Dose adjustments are made in 12.5–25 mcg increments based on TSH results, with rechecks every 6 weeks after changes. Long-term monitoring occurs every 6–12 months. Options B, C, and D contain incorrect timing, dose increments, or monitoring intervals.
